titleStmt.title: Letter from James Davidson to Clara Davidson, 9 June 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: James Davidson

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Letter from James Davidson (1877-1916) to his mother, Clara Mary (b.1852). James writes to thank his mother for his congratulations on James' engagement, agreeing that he and Eileen will be very happy together. James hopes to request another lot of leave, but doubts the possibility in the near future. He briefly mentions the death of British Secretary of State for War, Lord Kitchener (1850-1916), describing it as a great blow to the army at a critical time. He closes with a description of life at the front, that the weather has been terrible and many of the men have no bedding or changes of clothing.
This letter is part of a large correspondence from Captain James Davidson to his parents. Davidson, who was Managing Director of the Sirocco Engineering Works in Belfast, served with 13 Royal Irish Rifles and the Machine Gun Corps. His letters describe his experiences while training in England and serving in France. He was killed on 1 July 1916, the first day of the Battle of the Somme.

msIdentifier.repository: Public Record Office of Northern Ireland

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
9/6/16 My dearest Mother Ever so
many thanks for your
dear kind letters about
my engagement to Eileen.
Yes we have been such good chums & known
each other so long that
I feel sure we shall
be very happy together
when the time comes.
Just wish I could see
some possibility of
getting away for a
second leave soon —
I fear it is very much
in the dim distance.
My Coy did not go
back with the Brigade
as we of course expected to
but remained where we
were & I think it is now
very unlikely we shall
be relieved at all.
Personally I really don't
much mind for unless
one goes back a long
way it means very little
rest and to me the line is
much more interesting.
am feeling better tho
last few days— it was
just a slight chill I had
— but weather is quite
depressing, rain every
day and quite cold. The
poor men have had
rather a bad time of
it as all their blankets
were withdrawn and they
only have their greatcoats
for extra coverings at
nights. Of course they
sleep in their clothes
but they get no stray
or anything to lie on
so it is not a comfortable
bed they have. Just
wish some of those lazy
strikers at home could
be put into similar
quarters. What a
sad thing that was
about Lord Kitchener
and what a great blow
to the Army at such
a critical time! The lining for my
waterproof has just
arrived — thanks
dear mother for
sending it. Will
post back the other
one. The parcel of
shirts also arrived
safely. Am so
glad you seem to be
happily fixed up in
the kitchen dept. Do
hope you won't have
any more trouble.
Now dear Mother Finish
Close. With my fondest
love
your loving son Jim
On Active Service. Written 9th June Wed 14th Mrs. S.C. Davidson
Seacourt
Bangor
Co Down. Ireland Jim Davidson
